name: Addition with Regrouping (Carrying)
whatIsIt: Sometimes when we add, a place overflows: 7 ones + 5 ones = 12 ones — too many for the ones room! Regrouping fixes it: we bundle 10 of those ones into 1 new ten and CARRY it to the tens room. That little carried 1 is not magic — it is a real bundle of ten moving house.
whyNeeded: Small additions fit on fingers, but real life adds bigger numbers: two shopping bills, two cricket scores, pages read this week and last week. Regrouping is what lets column addition work for ANY numbers — it is the engine inside every big addition.
realLifeUses: Money: a ₹47 notebook and a ₹25 pencil box — total bill?
Cricket: 38 runs in the first innings, 26 in the second
Reading: 56 pages last week + 38 pages this week
Steps: walking 68 steps to the shop and 47 steps back home

vocabulary.meaning: Bundle 10 ones into 1 ten (or 10 tens into 1 hundred).
Move the new bundle to the next place on the left.
Adding numbers written one under the other, place by place.

story.title: The Grain Merchant's Fair Count
story.text: At the market, a grain merchant pours 47 sugar grains into a bowl, then 25 more. 'How many now?' asks Ravi. 'Add the loose ones first: 7 and 5 make 12 — but 12 is more than ten! Ten of them make one full TEN, and 2 stay loose,' says the merchant, tying ten into a bundle. 'That new ten joins the rest: 4 tens, 2 tens, and 1 carried ten make 7 tens. So 72 grains in all.'
story.extractedProblem: 47 sugar grains + 25 sugar grains. How many grains in total?
story.answerInStory: 72 grains — the 12 loose ones became 1 new ten and 2 singles, so 7 tens and 2 ones.

visual.caption: 7 ones + 5 ones = 12 ones → tape 10 into a NEW ten-rod, keep 2 singles. Tens: 4 + 2 + 1(new) = 7. Answer: 72.

teachingGallery.title: Carrying — when the ones make a ten
teachingGallery.note: Add the ones first. If they reach ten or more, carry a ten to the next column.

examples.caption: Ones make 12 → that's 1 ten and 2 ones. CARRY the ten.
2 tens + 1 ten + carried 1 ten = 4 tens; 2 ones left → 42.

standardMethod.summary: Add the ones first. If they make 10 or more, carry the new ten to the tens column.
steps: Write the numbers one under the other, ones under ones, tens under tens.
Add the ONES column: 7 + 5 = 12. Write 2, carry the 1 (a real ten!) above the tens.
Add the TENS column with the carry: 4 + 2 + 1 = 7.
Read the answer: 72. Check: does it look about right? 47 + 25 is near 50 + 25 = 75 ✓

mentalMathMethod.name: Left-to-right adding
steps: Add the TENS first (or biggest place).
Then add the ones.
Combine — adjust if the ones make a new ten.

alternateMethods.name: Blocks method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE what the carried 1 really is.
steps: Build both numbers with ten-rods and one-cubes.
Push all the ones together; every time 10 cubes gather, trade them for 1 rod.
Count rods and leftover cubes — that is the sum.
alternateMethods.example: 47 + 25: cubes 7+5=12 → trade 10 for a rod → 7 rods, 2 cubes → 72.

alternateMethods.name: Number-line jumps
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When you like hopping instead of columns.
steps: Start at the bigger number: 47.
Jump the tens of the other number: 47 → 57 → 67 (that's +20).
Jump the ones: 67 → 72 (that's +5).

alternateMethods.name: Vedic: digit-sum check (Gunitasamuccayah)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To CHECK an addition is right — add up the digits (casting out nines).
steps: Add the digits of each number down to one digit: 47 → 4+7 = 11 → 1+1 = 2; 38 → 3+8 = 11 → 2.
Add those digit-sums: 2 + 2 = 4.
Now digit-sum the answer 85 → 8+5 = 13 → 1+3 = 4.
Both give 4, so 47 + 38 = 85 checks out.

workedExamples.problem: A shop sold 38 laddus in the morning and 26 in the evening. How many laddus in the whole day?
steps: Column setup: 38 over 26, places aligned.
Ones: 8 + 6 = 14 → write 4, carry 1 ten.
Tens: 3 + 2 + 1(carry) = 6.
Total: 64 laddus. Estimate check: 40 + 26 = 66 — close ✓

workedExamples.problem: Ravi added 47 + 25 and wrote 612. 'Ones made 12, so I wrote 12!' What went wrong?
steps: 12 ones cannot ALL live in the ones column — only one digit fits per place.
Regroup: 12 ones = 1 ten + 2 ones. Write the 2, CARRY the 1.
Tens: 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 → answer 72, not 612.
Estimate check catches it too: 47 + 25 must be near 70, and 612 is way off!
workedExamples.answer: 72 — write one digit, carry the bundle.

trickPractice.intro: When the ones pile past 9, bundle ten of them and carry it to the tens column.

hintLadder: Add the ones: 7 + 5 = 12. Write 2, carry 1 ten.
Add the ones: 8 + 6 = 14. Write 4, carry 1 ten.
Add the ones: 6 + 9 = 15. Write 5, carry 1 ten.
Add the ones: 7 + 8 = 15. Write 5, carry 1 ten.
Add the ones: 9 + 4 = 13. Write 3, carry 1 ten.
Add the ones: 5 + 8 = 13. Write 3, carry 1 ten.
Add the ones: 6 + 7 = 13. Write 3, carry 1 ten.
Add the ones: 8 + 5 = 13. Write 3, carry 1 ten.

commonMistakes.mistake: Regrouping the ones but forgetting to add the carried 1 in the tens (47 + 25 = 62).
commonMistakes.fix: The carry is a REAL ten waiting upstairs! Write it clearly above the tens column and greet it first: 'carry says 1... plus 4 plus 2.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing the whole 12 in the ones column (47 + 25 = 612).
commonMistakes.fix: One digit per room! When ones make 12, split it: 2 stays home, the 1 (a bundle of ten) moves to the tens shelf — Addy's warehouse rule.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing 47 + 5 with the 5 under the 4, adding wrong columns together.
commonMistakes.fix: Line up from the RIGHT: ones under ones, tens under tens. A quick grid or squared paper keeps every digit in its lane.

easy.q: Addy's warehouse gets 26 grains + 17 grains. How many in total?
hintLadder: 13 ones! Keep 3, tape 10 into a new rod — carry 1.

easy.q: When the ones column makes 14, what do you write and carry?
hintLadder: 14 ones = how many tens and how many ones?
The ones digit stays; the ten travels left.

medium.q: A ₹47 notebook and a ₹25 pencil box. What is the total bill in rupees?
hintLadder: Check with an estimate: about 50 + 25 = 75, so your answer should be close.

medium.q: Try the friendly-ten trick in your head: 38 + 26 = ?
hintLadder: 38 needs 2 to become 40. Borrow the 2 from 26.

challenge.q: Ravi wrote 47 + 25 = 62. Which warehouse rule did he break?

options.label: He made the new ten but forgot to add the carry in the tens
He wrote both digits of 12 in the ones

challenge.answer: He made the new ten but forgot to add the carry in the tens
hintLadder: His ones digit (2) is right — so the regrouping started well.
Tens: 7 + 5 + 1 = 13 tens! That is 1 hundred and 3 tens.
Write 3 in the tens, and the new hundred in front: 134.
challenge.explain: Regrouping works at every level: 10 ones → 1 ten, 10 tens → 1 hundred.

questions.q: The little 1 we carry to the tens column is really…

options.label: A bundle of ten ones moving to the tens place

questions.answer: A bundle of ten ones moving to the tens place
hintLadder: Think of Addy taping grains together.
One ten-rod, moved to the tens shelf.

questions.q: Two innings: 38 runs + 46 runs. Total score?
Pages read: 56 last week + 38 this week = ?
Before trusting your answer, Addy always does one last check. Which?

options.label: An estimate: is the answer close to a friendly round sum?
Nothing — column addition never goes wrong

questions.answer: An estimate: is the answer close to a friendly round sum?
hintLadder: If you got 612, would the estimate warn you?
Yes! Estimation is the answer's bodyguard.

teachBackPrompt: You are Addy's new warehouse manager! Explain the warehouse rule to someone at home: what happens when the ones shelf collects 12 grains, and why the carried 1 must never be forgotten. Show them with 47 + 25.

revisionCard.summary: Add ones first. 10 or more? REGROUP: keep the ones digit, CARRY the new ten left (it's a real bundle!). Then add the tens including the carry. Always estimate-check: 47+25 ≈ 75, so 72 ✓ and 612 ✗.

gameMission.title: The Sugar Warehouse Rush
gameMission.brief: Deliveries are pouring in! Combine each pair of sugar shipments, tape every 10 loose grains into rods, and keep Addy's shelves perfect. Beware: Ravi keeps hiding the carry!

realLifeProject: Shop bill detective: take a real shop bill (or make one with a parent) with two items under ₹100. Add them with columns, then check with the friendly-ten trick. Do both ways give the same total?

abacusMethod.name: Carry a bead to the next rod
abacusMethod.whenToUse: To add with regrouping on the abacus.
steps: Add on the ones rod. If it fills past nine, clear ten and carry ONE bead to the tens rod.
Add the tens (plus any carried bead).
Read the answer across the rods.
abacusMethod.example: 27 + 18: ones fill past ten → carry 1 ten → 45.

visual.caption: Ones overflow → carry one bead to the tens rod.

vedicMethod.name: Complete the whole (Vedic)
vedicMethod.whenToUse: When one number is just below a ten/hundred.
steps: Round the near number up to the next ten or hundred.
Add — this is easy.
Subtract the small amount you added extra.

rememberIt.hook: When a column makes 10 or more, carry the ten to the next column
rememberIt.unpack: Add the ones; if 10 or more, write the ones digit and carry 1 ten. Work right to left.

studentQuestions.a: When a column adds to 10+, the extra ten carries to the next column left. 8+5=13: write 3, carry 1.

studentQuestions.q: Which column do I start with?
studentQuestions.a: The ones (rightmost), then move left, so carries land in a column you have not added yet.
